meta.title: Mercedes-Benz M276/M278 Engine — Specs, Reliability & Replacement Cost

hero.h1: Mercedes-Benz M276/M278 Engine — Specs, Reliability & Replacement Cost
hero.subHeadline: The Mercedes M276 is a 3.5L V6 (or 4.7L V8 twin-turbo M278) — silky smooth with strong torque, but timing chain wear and oil leaks are notable. Backed by real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB-INTERVIEW].

rows.value: Confidence: 88% confidence — based on UK specialist feedback and owner-reported data across a broad range of models.

rows.metric: Mix of well-understood repairs (timing chain, oil leaks) and V-engine complexity

rows.value: Good parts availability from Mercedes and aftermarket; 4.7L V8 has strong support

rows.metric: Reconditioned cost 30–44% of lowest host vehicle value

rows.value: Sum of the five rows above

rows.metric: One-line verdict: The M276/M278 are refined, torquey V6/V8s — timing chain stretch and oil leaks are the main watch-points — buy with full service history and inspect chain health.
rows.value: Best for: Buyers wanting a smooth, powerful petrol Mercedes with modern features.

rows.metric: Avoid if: You're not prepared for potential timing chain or V-engine oil leak costs.

The M276 (3.5L V6) and M278 (4.7L V8 twin-turbo) were introduced to replace the M272 and M273 engines, bringing direct injection, improved efficiency, and significant torque increases. The M278 features twin turbochargers mounted in the V of the engine, reducing lag and improving packaging.
* Smooth, refined power delivery — both engines deliver strong torque from low revs
rows.value: * Direct injection — improves efficiency and power

rows.metric: * Twin-turbo M278 — near-instant response with 402–577 hp
rows.value: * Wide application — fitted to core Mercedes models: E, S, CLS, ML, GLE, GLS, SL, etc.

rows.metric: * Better reliability than earlier M272/M273 — timing chain issues exist but are less catastrophic
* Timing chain stretch — M276 timing chain can stretch, leading to warning lights and potential engine damage if ignored. Symptoms include rattle on cold start.
rows.value: * Camshaft adjuster wear — related to timing chain issues, causing VVT faults.

rows.metric: * Oil leaks — V-engine valley leaks, valve cover gaskets, rear main seal.
rows.value: * High-pressure fuel pump — can fail, particularly on M278 V8.

rows.metric: * Turbocharger oil supply — M278 can suffer from turbo bearing wear if oil changes are skipped.
rows.value: * Thermostat housing — plastic housing can crack, causing coolant leaks.

The M276/M278 represent a significant step forward in refinement and power, while being more reliable than their predecessors. Regular oil changes and proactive chain inspection are the key to longevity.

costGuide.labourEstimate: Fitting an M276/M278 typically takes 12–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [ESTIMATED].

cta.label: → Compare M276/M278 engine prices from UK specialists

items.question: How much does an M276/M278 engine replacement cost?
items.answer: A reconditioned M276/M278 typically costs £3,500–£6,500 supply only [ESTIMATED], varying by V6 or V8 spec and whether turbos are included. Fitting adds £780–£1,300 depending on labour rates and model.

items.question: Is the M276/M278 a reliable engine?
items.answer: The M276/M278 rate 4.0★ out of 5 based on specialist feedback and UK owner data [MB-INTERVIEW]. They are refined and generally robust, but timing chain wear and oil leaks are the main areas to monitor.

items.question: What is the most common M276/M278 failure?
items.answer: Timing chain stretch is the most common reported issue, particularly on higher-mileage M276 V6s. Oil leaks from the V‑engine valley and valve cover gaskets are also frequent as the engines age.

items.question: Which Mercedes models use the M276/M278?
items.answer: The M276/M278 powers E‑Class, S‑Class, CLS, ML/GLE, GLS, GL, SL, G‑Class, and some C‑Class and AMG models — covering a wide range from the early 2010s to around 2020.

items.question: How long does the M276/M278 last?
items.answer: With regular oil changes and proactive timing chain inspection, the M276/M278 routinely exceeds 150,000 miles. Many specialists report V8 M278 examples at 200,000+ with proper maintenance [MB-INTERVIEW].
